He narrates that the prophet Sam said. This narration was collected by Abu Dud which translates roughly to a man is not to be asked about his reprimand of his wife. Now this narration is not an authentic narration. It has weakness in its chain similar to the weakness found in the narration of Abu Bakr that we took before. weak. This weakness is slight, but notice what Noah always says here. I'm going to give you this point and I want you to remember it. Okay? If we were to say, what are the six references of hadith? What are the six what are referred to as the references of the books of what are they? Can we mention them? les alkari Muslim Abu DB. Okay. So those are the six books. If you hear a scholar say the seven books, it includes Musad Ahmed. If you hear them say the eight books, it includes but usually it is the six books. Okay, these six books, why are they called the six mother books of hadith or the six references or the six the six references. The reason is when we take all the narrations in these books and we take all of the mut meaning the words that are ascribed as being the prophet's words, the prophet's actions, the prophet's anything having to do with him. It becomes part of the sunnah and we remove repetition. Okay? And we remove chains of narration. This gives us the bulk majority of all of the prophet sunnah. Not all of it, not every last narration, not every last statement or action, but the bulk majority. Okay, this makes up the bulk majority of the prophet sunnah. The first scholar to consider these six books in this way is and after that all of the scholars followed in suit. Now these books are not all authentic compilations. Books of hadith are of different types. Imagine imagine tools. The author intended this to be a tool for a specific task. Bkari and Muslim are authentic compilations. What does that mean? It means he compiled in his book. Every narration every narration in that book he is grading as authentic. Muslim did the same thing. Everything in this book is authentic according to the following set of criteria. Now the rest of the sun these are a different type of book are these books are referred to as what is a book of sunn it's a type of book of hadith book of sunnen is a book that essentially deals with aim rulings, okay? Rulings, woo, salah, fasting, etc. All the way through jihad, inheritance, from the beginning to the end, this is what it is dealing with. Now, the author of a sunn his job is to is to u compile compile as many as many sunen which is plural for sunnah as many sun as he can and then he'll mention a chapter. So for instance he'll say the book of salah then he'll mention chapters in that book this is the book of the times of salah this is the or this is the chapter of the time of the chapter of the time of the chapter of the time of etc. You get the point. And the point here is that the narration he'll mention is going to be the strongest narration in that subject and he will mention in his book as many said rulings and sunnen as he can whatever the scholars differ on. Right? So for instance, one of the things that the scholars have have mentioned, the jurists of Islam have mentioned is not using siwek, not brushing one's teeth. If you're fasting after the sun crosses noon, after noon, after the sun crosses its zenith, before you use after you don't, this is an opinion, okay? It's a it's a it's a claim made by some jurists. Where do they get this from? Well, they get this generally from the narration of the prophet sallall.aii wasallam that the smell of the stomach of a person who is fasting is more beloved to Allah than the scent of musk. We know this narration, correct? And so they say, when does this smell occur? It occurs most prominently after the first half of the day. Therefore, if a person brushes his teeth, he gets rid of this smell. No, we should keep the smell. This is a weak opinion. It is a weak opinion, but it's an opinion. So, what will the scholar who's writing a book on Sunn do? He'll mention that opinion and then he'll mention the strongest evidence used by jurists as an evidence for that opinion. He's not saying the opinion is correct. He's not even claiming the narration is cor is authentic. He is merely documenting this field the field of the field of. Is he saying that every time he he he documents an irration, is he saying it's authentic? No. No. He's documenting opinions and their evidences. That's it. Doesn't mean everything in the book is authentic. He is going to mention the strongest narration in that field. The strongest narration in that issue might be weak. The strongest narration in that issue might be very weak. We understand that. Therefore, the scholars of hadith have different types of books that they write. This narration we just heard is from Sunb Noahi died at what age? Anyone know? Mid4s. 45. Exactly. 45 years old. and he started officially seeking knowledge at the age of 18. The spirit of the narration is the human being, a couple, a man and his wife, they have a right to privacy. Prying into that is not the right of any other person. It is the right of the husband, the right of the wife. If there is a wrongdoing, then the proper channels for that is the court, the Muslim court. There are to be notified and brought into the situation and they then will investigate what is going on in that household. However, Tom, Dick, and Harry and the guy walking outside and the guy, this is none of the none of their business. People have an innate inherent right to privacy that is part of their dignity. And a man is the the sultan or the caretaker and the superior in his household. He has a right to that privacy and he has a right to that discretion and a mother with her children and so forth. Therefore, it is impermissible for a person to pry into those things. And if a person does, they are overstepping their bounds. Why does that fall into the chapter of vigilance of Allah? You know what? I heard this and I heard

summary: This lecture discusses the ninth narration in the chapter on vigilance of Allah's observation from a hadith collection. The instructor examines a weak hadith from Sunan Abu Dawud regarding privacy in marital matters, explaining that a man should not be questioned about his discipline of his wife. The lecture provides important context about the different types of hadith books, distinguishing between authentic compilations (like Sahih Bukhari and Sahih Muslim) and books of Sunan, which document various scholarly opinions and their evidences. The instructor explains that Abu Dawud's authenticity standards were lower than other scholars, resulting in some weak narrations in his collection, though the meanings of these narrations remain correct. The spiritual essence of this particular narration emphasizes the right to privacy in household matters and the proper channels (courts, elders) for addressing domestic concerns rather than gossip or prying.
suggested_title: Privacy Rights in Islam and Understanding Hadith Book Classifications
hadith_chapter: Chapter of Vigilance of Allah's Observation
